title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who attended a traditional woodworking course. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: The Art of the Grain: Traditional Woodworking

texts.A:
### Liam, The Cabinet Maker
'I spent twenty years behind a desk, so working with my hands was a revelation,' Liam says. [51]The hardest part was the precision required; a mistake of one millimeter could ruin an entire week's work.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my chronic wrist strain from typing completely disappeared after I learned correct tool technique.[/48] [53]I learned that wood isn't just a material; it’s a living thing that you have to listen to and respect.[/53] [50]I realized that my background as an architect helped me visualize the three-dimensional joints much faster than others.[/50] [47]Initially, the lack of power tools was a shock, but I eventually grew to love the quiet rhythm of the hand-saw.[/47] It was a grounding experience.
texts.B:
### Sarah, The Furniture Restorer
Sarah focused on repairing antique chairs. 'It’s about honoring the original maker,' she explains. [49]The experience taught me that true sustainability lies in making things that can be repaired for centuries.[/49] [52]I hadn't anticipated the emotional weight of working on a family heirloom that had been neglected for decades.[/52] [47]I was quite startled by how quickly I adapted to the physical demands of standing at a workbench all day.[/47] [54]What I valued most was the patience of the master carpenter, who never once lost his temper at my mistakes.[/54] [56]If I hadn't seen a flyer at my local hardware store, I never would have known the course existed.[/56] It was a creative awakening.
texts.C:
### Victor, The Bowl Turner
Victor learned to use a traditional pole-lathe. 'It’s a physical dance,' he notes. [51]I struggled with the rhythmic coordination required between my foot and my hands, which I found incredibly frustrating at first.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general patience and concentration levels reached an all-time high, which was a great mental side effect.[/48] [53]I realized that the flaws in the wood are what give the final piece its character and soul.[/53] [50]I found that the focus I used in my previous career as a pilot was perfect for the absolute concentration required at the lathe.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the wood dust and the physical mess, which I found quite repulsive initially.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried I was too clumsy, but the wood taught me grace.
texts.D:
### Elena, The Timber Framer
Elena worked on building a traditional barn. 'It was like a giant puzzle for adults,' she laughs. [49]The project made me realize that we’ve lost a vital connection to the structures we inhabit.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have found the apprenticeship if my brother hadn't recommended it after his own experience there.[/56] [54]I was moved by the camaraderie among the diverse group of students, who supported each other through the long days.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the physical exhaustion of working outdoors in the heat to be so draining initially.[/47] [55]I found the initial weeks of learning complex geometric layouts incredibly tedious and confusing.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that I wouldn't be strong enough, but teamwork solved every heavy lifting problem.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by how a physical or environmental factor affected their initial progress?
questions.explanation: Liam (A) mentions the lack of power tools, and Elena (D) mentions the exhaustion of working in the heat.

questions.question: notes a positive physical or mental health change that resulted from the manual work?
questions.explanation: Liam (A) found his wrist strain disappeared, and Victor (C) saw his concentration levels reach an all-time high.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perception regarding sustainability or our relationship with objects?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) reflects on sustainability and repair, and Elena (D) on our connection to buildings.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or skills were advantageous during the course?
questions.explanation: Liam (A) used his architect background, and Victor (C) used his pilot's focus.

questions.question: describes a specific technical or physical coordination challenge that was difficult to master?
questions.explanation: Liam (A) mentions the millimeter precision, and Victor (C) the coordination of foot and hands.

questions.question: admits to having had prior concerns or having an unexpected emotional response to the work?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) felt the emotional weight of a family heirloom, and Victor (C) worried about being clumsy.

questions.question: reflects on a deeper philosophical lesson learned about the nature of the material or its flaws?
questions.explanation: Liam (A) learned wood is a living thing, and Victor (C) saw flaws as character.

questions.question: highlights the value of the guidance or the camaraderie found during the sessions?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) valued the master carpenter's patience, and Elena (D) the camaraderie of the students.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the technical or physical conditions?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) adjusted to the wood dust, and Elena (D) found the geometry layouts tedious.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external source or recommendation that led them to the course?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) saw a flyer in a hardware store, and Elena (D) was recommended by her brother.
